texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The iron and steel industry, after the cement industry, is the second most important in modern society in terms of production volumes, and it is the fi rst in terms of direct CO2 emissions. Iron and steel industry emissions in 2006 accounted for 30% of the 7.2 Gt of direct CO2 industrial emissions, followed by the cement industry which was responsible for 26% [1]. Overall, these CO2 emissions account for 5 -7% of the total anthropogenic emissions [2,3]. Steel production has increased rapidly since the beginning of the century and this trend is expected to continue. Fig. 1.1 presents the reported annual global steel production.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Worldwide steel production is still mainly achieved from primary manufacturing by carbon-intensive processes in integrated steel mills, making this industry the fi rst in terms of direct CO2 emissions. Both carbon capture and storage (CCS) and carbon capture and utilization (CCU) approaches are currently considered to o ff er a solution to the high carbon-footprint of primary steel production. Design of available or development of new gas separation-puri fi cation technologies are at the heart of these strategies, and often represent the largest share of the total project cost. This work presents the current state of development of the main technologies that have shown potential thus far at pilot or industrial scale for the treatment of gases within the steelmaking industry. An analysis of the opportunities and limitations of each technology is presented, related to their ability to separate existing gas streams into the two main carbon-bearing species, CO2 and CO. Recovery of H2, available in important quantities, is also considered. Main results from previous and ongoing research are presented and analyzed to draw a picture of the current situation, and o ff er key points for future development.

texts.orig: The BF-BOF process is more energy intensive than the EAF process because of the energy needs associated with iron ore reduction in the blast furnace, and with the conditioning of the raw materials, iron ore agglomeration and coking processes. It consumes on average 13 -14 GJ/ ton of steel whereas the EAF process consumes on average 4 -6 GJ/ton of steel [8]. Thereby, CO2 emissions are also higher for the BF-BOF process than for EAF process, 1.3 -1.6 ton CO2/ton of steel vs. 0.3 -0.5 ton CO2/ton of steel [9]. These ranges take into account factors such as energy e ffi ciency and the percentage of fossil energy used which depend on local conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions from steel production by ISM ' s has been the topic of extensive research worldwide. Several alternative ultra-low-CO2 steel production technologies are under development to replace existing carbon-based steel manufacturing [10]. Additionally, CCS, and more recently CCU, are being studied with the objective of reducing the environmental impact of existing carbonbased producing technologies before the implementation of zero carbon emission steel production. Gas separation technologies for the capture step play a vital role in the storage or valorization schemes, and are key parts of all of the ongoing research e ff orts in the fi eld. ISM ' s gases unlike fl ue gases from power production, have the particularity that besides CO2 and N2 other gas species are present at important concentrations, notably CO, H2 and CH4. The concentration of these species varies within the available residuary gases as described in the following section. This work aims to provide a comprehensive overview of available results of the application of gas absorption, gas adsorption, and membrane separation technologies for the recovery of the aforementioned gas species. Key features, drawbacks and research directions for the use of each technology are given based on the data collected. Cost information, when available, is given to help readers trying to fi nd the most cost-e ff ective technology for a given separation involving ISM ' gases. A complete cost comparison between technologies is not feasible given the large di ff erences in process speci fi cations, separation media and cost parameters among available results. Readers can however use the information here to quickly identify and select potential technologies adapted to their needs that can later be compared within their own technical and economical scenarios.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Integrated steel mills and gas emissions
texts.text: 2. Integrated steel mills and gas emissions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An integrated steel mill is composed of several interconnected units due to the di ff erent steps involved in steel production but also due to the recycling of residuary gases within the process itself for sensible heat recovery and/or power generation. These hot gases are the coke oven gas (COG), the blast furnace gas (BFG), and the basic oxygen furnace gas (BOFG). The lower heating value of the COG is the highest of the three with around 5 kWh/m 3 , followed by the BOFG with around 3 kWh/m 3 , and with a low comparative value, BFG with around 0.9 kWh/m 3 . Fig. 2.1 presents a simpli fi ed fl ow diagram illustrating the way these gases are typically used within the steel mill. Most mills use the COG to heat the air necessary for the blast furnace and the ovens of the sinter plant and lime kiln, and a part is recycled back for the coke ovens. It is also used to fuel boilers to supply steam for electricity generation and sometimes for fi nishing processes downstream. In general, 15% is used in the coke ovens, 15% is used for power generation and the rest is divided between the other described units [5]. Remaining COG is in some cases burnt o ff in torches and even directly emitted to the atmosphere [3].

texts.orig: CO2 is the center of attention when discussing emissions in the iron and steel industry. On one hand it is the component with the highest level of net emissions within the species present in an integrated steel mill, and on the other hand it is the main component considered for cleaner production metrics from a greenhouse gas production view. However, other valuable gas components are present along with CO2 on the three main residuary gases cited above, namely H2, CO, and CH4. The proportion among these species is rather di ff erent for the three emissions as illustrated in Table 2.2, which is at the origin of their di ff erences in calori fi c value and hence their use within the steel plant. Blast furnaces are operated at high pressure to increase productivity, and the pressure in the exiting BFG is used to drive top pressure recovery turbines that allow for the production of electricity resulting in energy savings that can be of the order of 30% of the electricity necessary for all the BF equipment [5]. BFG could be taken for gas separation before this energy recovery step to pro fi t from the higher partial pressure of the gas species. In such a case, an energy balance must be done to evaluate if the added e ffi ciency in the capture process compensates the energy no longer recovered in the turbines.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Carbon capture and storage (CCS) and carbon capture and utilization (CCU) e ff orts in the iron and steel sector
texts.text: 3. Carbon capture and storage (CCS) and carbon capture and utilization (CCU) e ff orts in the iron and steel sector

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The International Energy Agency has constantly highlighted the importance of CO2 capture and storage as a future option for signi fi cant GHG ' s emissions reduction from the Iron and Steel sector to the 2050 horizon. Increases in energy e ffi ciency where possible, greater recycling of metal scrap, and the use of emerging cleaner primary steel production technologies must also play a role along with CCS by 2020 if reduction objectives are to be met [1,12]. More recently, carbon capture and utilization (CCU) has also been suggested as a potential alternative for emissions reduction by means of the re-use of CO2 and/or CO generated during steel manufacturing [13 -18].

texts.orig: -Geosphere sinks, these are naturally occurring sinks that require anthropogenic intervention in order to be used. Examples of sinks include oil reservoirs suitable for enhanced oil recovery (EOR), depleted oil and gas reservoirs, coal beds and deep aquifers. These are the type of sinks that are used during CCS.

texts.orig: -Material sinks, these are carbon sinks created or designed by man. Examples include durable wood products, chemicals, fuels, biomass and plastics among others. These are the type of sinks that are referred to when discussing CCU.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clearly there are major di ff erences between these sinks when it comes to selection and operation under an industrial emissions reduction perspective. Parameters that should be considered include [19]: environmental impact of the proposed sink mechanism, sink CO2 capacity, retention/residence time of CO2 in the sink, potential for accelerated leakage of CO2, rate of CO2 uptake, validation of sequestration in the sink, suitability of the sink relative to the emission source and type, and cost of implementation/utilization of the sink mechanism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Biosphere sinks have been questioned so far for carbon storage projects from concentrated industrial sources due to factors such as possible disruption of natural ecosystems around the injection points, possible instability of the CO2 as in the case of ocean fl oors, and veri fi cation of o ff sets in carbon emissions [19,20]. Biosphere sinks are not considered as viable for direct CO2 storage from industrial emissions. The main e ff orts made by the iron and steel industry to reduce the overall emissions are then CCS applications using geosphere sinks or, more recently, CCU using materials sinks. The most relevant projects are presented in the following sections.

The most relevant projects are presented in the following sections.

texts.orig: The only project in the steelmaking industry that has been reported as having moved into commercial scale is the project of Emirates Steel Industries (ESI) under the Al Reyadah joint venture between ESI and Abu Dhabi ' s national oil company (ADNOC). The 122 million US$ project will sequester up to 800,000 tons of CO2 annually. Operation started in November 2016. CO2 will be captured onsite at the country ' s largest steelmaking facility and it will be compressed and transported along a 43 km pipeline to be injected into oils fi elds to enhance oil recovery while storing the injected CO2 underground. It is worth noting that in this project the CO2 is generated by direct reduced iron technology using natural gas and not by a blast furnace. Amine absorption technology is used for CO2 separation and enrichment since high purity is needed for injection into the oil reservoirs [23]. This project can be considered a hybrid of CCU and CCS as the CO2 is being used as an enhanced recovery agent in the reservoir at the same time that it is stored underground. Projects that have mainly a CCU perspective are presented in the following section.

texts.orig: Recent simulation studies have been published dealing with technology assessments for CCU applications speci fi c to the iron and steel industry, highlighting the potential of such approach. Arvola et al. [18] considered the hypothetical utilization of CO from BFG and BOFG for the production of chemical products. In their analysis, CO rich emissions are sold to chemical producers installed in the proximity of the steel plant, gas separation and treatment costs are at the expense of the chemical actors and are thus not considered in the economic calculations. Others assumptions include a 88% recovery of a 99% pure CO stream by a vacuum swing adsorption process, CO2 emissions trade costs of 20 € /ton CO2, electricity prices of 40 € /MWh coming from nonfossil sources, and CO selling prices of 100 € /kNm 3 based on estimated price information from coal and biomass gasi fi cation processes. Under such scenario of trade between a steel plant and an exterior chemical producer, authors estimated a positive economic impact of 44 M € /year from a plant producing 2125 MNm 3 /year of BFG and 212.5 MNm 3 /year of BOFG.

texts.orig: For a supposed steel production rate of 150 -170 tons of liquid steel/ h and carbon dioxide emission and sequestration costs of 52 $ and 26 $/ton CO2 respectively, the optimal operation of the process was found to be at maximum steel production with a calculated net present value (NPV) of 2.04 G$. No electricity or district heat are produced during optimal operation and 18.8 tons of methanol per hour are produced from a mix of 199.1 kNm 3 /h of BFG with 6.2 kNm 3 /h of BOFG. The polygeneration structure also resulted in lower speci fi c CO2 emissions of 0.97 ton CO2/ton liquid steel compared to the conventional steel plant without polygeneration producing 1.6 -1.7 ton CO2/ton liquid steel. [17]. Interestingly, the e ff ect of changes in the supposed values of CO2 emission and sequestration costs as well as in material and product costs on the optimization results was also evaluated. High CO2 emission costs (50 -150 $/ton CO2) and low sequestration costs (0 -50 $/ton CO2) led to the lowest speci fi c emissions ( ∼ 0.6 ton CO2 ton liquid steel) as an important part of the carbon is sent to sequestration. For higher sequestration costs the carbon is shifted to the methanol production and speci fi c emissions are increased. When the emission and sequestration costs are similar, the speci fi c emissions are rather constant at a value between 0.9 and 1 ton CO2 per ton liquid steel [17].

texts.orig: It is worth to mention that at an industrial level, outside of the iron and steel industry, several companies are developing the potential of CCU technologies within GHG ' s emissions reduction programs and are trying to fi nd ways of tackling the challenges cited above, with promising results. Carbon Recycling International (CRI) is a company that started their fi rst commercial demonstration plant of methanol synthesis from CO2 in Iceland in 2011, and today they o ff er commercial scale plants on a turn-key basis for methanol production from either renewable electricity and CO2 emissions or H2 rich gases and CO2 emissions. Their demonstration plant used renewable energy along with CO2 emissions from a geothermal power plant [16,29]. In the U.S., a public funded project, executed by Solidia Technologies, demonstrated a technology for the production of concrete cured with CO2 instead of water which can reduce carbon emissions up to 70% and recycle 60 -100% of the water used in production of concrete products [30]. The French company Lafarge, member of the global Lafarge-Holcim group, has established an agreement to develop the technology at an industrial scale and begin commercialization of the new concrete fi rst in the US and later in Europe. In the petrochemical industry, Saudi Arabia ' s Saudi Basic Industries Corp (SABIC) has constructed facilities allowing the separation and puri fi cation of CO2 emissions from the production of ethylene glycol, which CO2 content is ∼ 95%, and their utilization by an in-house developed and patented technology. Up to 1500 tons/day of CO2 can be puri fi ed and pipelined to other SABIC a ffi liates for the production of urea, methanol and even lique fi ed CO2 for the food and drink industry, making it the world ' s largest CO2 utilization plant [31]. A similar approach has been taken by Gulf Petrochemical Industries Company (GPIC) in Bahrain, whose emissions recovery plant, based on Mitsubishi Heavy Industries ' CO2 capture technology, can capture up to 450 tons/day of CO2 from the petrochemical plant for chemical and food and beverage applications [32].

texts.orig: In France, the Low Impact Steelmaking (LIS) program is the continuation of previous e ff orts for the reduction of the carbon footprint of steel manufacturing. The LIS program, launched in 2013, is the followup to the ULCOS project and is supported by the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME). Within the LIS program, the VALORCO project is studying potential uses of gas emissions from steel production, namely CO2, but also CO, CH4 and H2 which, as illustrated in Table 2.2, are present along with CO2 in the BFG-BOF steel production.
texts.text: In France, the Low Impact Steelmaking (LIS) program is the continuation of previous e ff orts for the reduction of the carbon footprint of steel manufacturing. The LIS program, launched in 2013, is the followup to the ULCOS project and is supported by the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME). Within the LIS program, the VALORCO project is studying potential uses of gas emissions from steel production, namely CO2, but also CO, CH4 and H2 which, as illustrated in Table 2.2, are present along with CO2 in the BFG-BOF steel production.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The goal of the VALORCO project is the reduction of carbon emissions of at least 30%, and the main focus is the reduction of CO2 emissions. Other carbonated species (CO and CH4) are also present in the overall carbon balance. For these species, the approach is analogous, and their use besides recirculation within the steel plant to be used as fuel or as reducing agents relies as well on their puri fi cation and on the formation of new products, like for CO2, but here mainly by chemical transformations. The varying compositions of the gases presented in Table 2.2 condition, in principle, the utilization approach to each of these sources. The BFG is the main source of CO2 emissions in the steel plant and hence it is the main candidate for CO2 valorization. It is also enriched by a comparable amount of CO, which could be separated and returned to the blast furnace, as in the case of the TGR-BF technology (Fig. 3.1), or used along with the CO2 for the synthesis of new products by chemical transformations. A review of thermochemical processes under such perspective has been recently presented by Uribe-Soto et al. [13]. The COG has the highest content of H2 and CH4 of the three gases, and valorization opportunities revolve around these two species. Bermudez et al. [3] have reviewed in a recent work the main technologies available for COG valorization. The BOFG has important amounts of CO, and some CO2 in a lower proportion and so valorization opportunities are shared with the blast furnace gas which volume is much higher.

texts.orig: As shown in the previous sections, separation technologies are at the heart of any gas valorization technology, and the selection of the appropriate process alternative for a given separation must take into account several issues such as feasibility of the desired separation, operation and maintenance requirements, safety and environmental concerns, and cost considerations. The following section presents brie fl y a state of the art of the main gas separation technologies that are available for separation/enrichment of the valuable species present in the steelmaking gases based on results from published works on the subject. A full review of every technology is out of the scope of this overview. However, the most relevant technologies are presented along with any available performance and cost information updated using the chemical engineering plant cost index of 556.8 (2015) when cost information is previous to 2011, more than 5 years older to the considered cost index, and then translating the price to USD if necessary to give a quick comparable fi gure. Exchange rates of 1.1 USD/euro and 8.72 × 10 -3 USD/yen were used. Original cost data can be retrieved in the cited references if a more rigorous cost update is to be performed. It

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Gas separation technologies, applications in CCS and CCU, and possibilities for the iron and steel process gases
texts.text: 4. Gas separation technologies, applications in CCS and CCU, and possibilities for the iron and steel process gases

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CCS and most of the CCU technologies share one key issue in common, industrial gas emissions are not used directly but must undergo a separation/puri fi cation step fi rst. CCS applications share among them rather similar separation targets because of the constraints imposed by the CO2 transport and storage stages. In principle, the gas streams could be transported and injected underground without the puri fi cation stage, however energy costs and other associated costs make this approach impractical [33]. It is therefore necessary to produce a high purity stream. De Visser et al. suggested a CO2 purity of at least 95.5%, and a 4% upper limit for the non-condensable components (N2, O2, H2, CH4 and Ar) from a transport speci fi cation perspective [34]. Other researchers present collected data of purity speci fi cations of captured streams for power plant applications. The values depend on the type of capture process [35]: 99.8 -99.97% in post-combustion CO2 capture scenarios, 95.6 -99.7% in pre-combustion scenarios, and from 85% to 99.94% in oxyfuel scenarios. They reported that impurity additions up to 2% (mol) did not a ff ect the relative cost/km compared to a pure CO2 equivalent but it does increase compression requirements and thus operational cost of the transport operation. Also, hydrogen in particular should be limited to less than 1% (mol) to reduce compression costs [35]. The e ff ect of impurities on CO2 storage has been recently treated within the CO2QUEST project [36] funded by the European Union. CO2 injection with impurities into shallow aquifers showed no signi fi cant impact on aquifer contamination, and simulation of storage into deep geological formations showed the role of potential impurities N2, CH4, and H2S to reduce convective mixing and enhanced solubility trapping of CO2 [36].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High purity requirements along with compression to a pressure suitable for pipeline transport help explain why the CO2 capture step is the largest component of the overall costs in the CCS technology for most large sources of CO2 [33,37]. For CCU applications, purity requirements of CO2 vary depending on the speci fi c utilization pathway and can range from high purity speci fi cations as in the case of EOR applications (95% CO2 minimum) [38] to cases where there are no minimum purities required from an operation perspective such as mineral carbonation and microalgal technologies. For these utilization alternatives, a separation/capture step could still be needed for the removal of unwanted or problematic impurities, such as NO for microalgal systems, and/or for operation with concentrated feeds to increase pro fi tability and overall process e ffi ciency. Furthermore, purity speci fi cations of other valuable components present in the fl ue gases such as CO, CH4, and H2 can also be set depending on the speci fi c emission and utilization pathway.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several gas separation technologies are available for the capture step, these can be grouped in the following broad process categories: chemical absorption, physical absorption, adsorption, cryogenics, and membrane separation. The broad characteristics of each technology along with results of their application to steelmaking gases based on available references are presented in the following sections. Many technologies could be listed in a general review and such is not the purpose here. This work gives priority to technologies that have been tested at pilot or industrial scale within the steelmaking industry or that have been proposed to be used in the steelmaking industry while having been tested in other gas separation applications. Some new technologies, and improvements to classical separations are referenced when very positive results have been reported or the technology could o ff er new perspectives for the recovered gas species.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Chemical absorption technologies
texts.text: 4.1. Chemical absorption technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chemical absorption systems consider the use of chemical solvents that react reversibly with a targeted gas component. This mechanism allows for a very selective separation compared to alternative technologies, and high purities and recoveries are generally achievable. Di ffi culties arise from the handling of the solvent systems, often exhibiting corrosive or volatile characteristics as well as from the regeneration of the saturated (loaded) absorbent necessary for economical operation of the separation. Di ff erent processes are used for CO2 and CO absorption at industrial level as presented in the following subsections. No absorption process is available at industrial scale for H2 recovery.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.1. CO2 absorption technologies
texts.text: 4.1.1. CO2 absorption technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most widely used chemical solvents are alkanolamines in aqueous solution. These are classi fi ed in three categories depending on the type of the amine functional group: primary such as monoethanolamine (MEA) and diglycolamine (DGA), secondary such as diethanolamine (DEA), and tertiary such as triethanolamine (TEA) and methyldiethanolamine (MDEA). Reaction rates, heats of reaction, and corrosive behavior are di ff erent for each group. Primary amines are the most reactive, they possess the highest heats of reaction and are usually the most corrosive. These properties are less pronounced for secondary, and tertiary amines respectively. It is important to specify that amines are not intrinsically corrosive, corrosive behavior results from the dissolved acidic species. Acid gas loading (e.g. mol CO2/mol amine) and temperature are usually considered as the most important factors a ff ecting corrosivity [39]. The typical operation of the industrial absorption illustrated in Fig. 4.1 consists of two stages: one absorption stage where the CO2 rich gas meets the solvent solution, and one desorption stage where the CO2 loaded solution from the absorption stage is heated in order to recover the CO2 in a concentrated gas stream. Lean solvent exiting the stripper is recycled back to the absorption stage while recovering heat from it in a heat exchanger allowing the heating of the rich solvent before entering the stripper stage.

The typical operation of the industrial absorption illustrated in Fig. 4.1 consists of two stages: one absorption stage where the CO2 rich gas meets the solvent solution, and one desorption stage where the CO2 loaded solution from the absorption stage is heated in order to recover the CO2 in a concentrated gas stream. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chemical absorption technology is the most mature technology for CO2 capture since it has been commercialized for more than 60 years. It has been applied for the sweetening of natural gas or syngas and other process streams and it has been the topic of intensive research over the past two decades for the treatment of fl ue gases from power plants and industry. Advantages of the use of this technology include [40]: High CO2 purities and recoveries can be achieved; it can be applied to streams with low CO2 content; mature technology with vast operation experience available, and available waste heat could be used for solvent regeneration. The main drawbacks identi fi ed for its application to fl ue gases are the following [41]: high equipment corrosion rate and environmental impact of amines; amine degradation by SO2, NO2, and O2 which causes higher absorbent makeup rates; high energy consumption during high temperature absorbent regeneration, and large equipment sizes for industrially relevant sources leading to high separation costs. Separation costs for CO2 recovery from BFG with 30 -40% CO2 based on MEA absorption have been estimated at 71.7 $/ton CO2 [42].

texts.orig: Along with MEA-based process, many new process concepts and solvents have reached the pilot plant stage for fl ue gases from power plants, namely coal- fi red plants and the technology is expected to be commercial within this decade for post-combustion carbon capture from these plants [43,51]. In the Iron and steel sector, the Al Reyadah CCS project, scheduled for late 2016, uses conventional MEA absorption to capture the CO2 generated by a direct reduction process in a plant from Emirates Steel Industries in Mussafah (UAE) [23]. Gas based direct reduction processes are less carbon intensive and their emissions are more concentrated in CO2 which allows for lower capture costs than for traditional steel mills [5]. Few information is available on this project.

texts.orig: Chemical absorption for CO2 separation in the steel industry is one of the tasks included in the VALORCO project. This task has been carried out by the French Petroleum Institute (IFP Energies Nouvelles). Three solvent systems initially developed for CO2 capture on coal power stations were tested at laboratory and small pilot scale: Hicapt TM process based on MEA 30 wt%, Hicapt +TM process based on MEA 40 wt%, and DMX TM process based on proprietary demixing solvents [54,55].
texts.text: Chemical absorption for CO2 separation in the steel industry is one of the tasks included in the VALORCO project. This task has been carried out by the French Petroleum Institute (IFP Energies Nouvelles). Three solvent systems initially developed for CO2 capture on coal power stations were tested at laboratory and small pilot scale: Hicapt TM process based on MEA 30 wt%, Hicapt +TM process based on MEA 40 wt%, and DMX TM process based on proprietary demixing solvents [54,55].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Out of the three technologies, the DMX TM process was shown to be the cheapest. This process is based on the use of speci fi c solvents that exhibit liquid-liquid phase separation above a critical solubility temperature (LCST). Absorption is operated under this temperature so that only one phase is present. The rich solvent is heated leading to the formation of a water rich phase with a very high CO2 loading and an amine rich phase with a very low CO2 loading. Only the water rich phase is sent to the stripper stage thus reducing the liquid fl ow rate and associated energy requirement [54]. The DMX solvent also o ff ers better thermal and chemical stability with very low corrosion rates which makes possible the stripper operation under high temperature and pressure. Comparatively lower capital costs result from operation under such conditions. A CO2 rich stream (99% CO2) allowing very high recoveries (>99.5%) can be obtained at pressures up to 6 bars [56]. Energy consumption of the DMX TM process is around 2.1 GJ/ton CO2 [54]. Solvent degradation is seven times lower than that of the MDEA [54] and CO was found to have very low additional impact, however as for all amine-based CO2 capture processes, degradation under longterm real gas operation and emissions are expected [55].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solvent used in the process is a copper salt, cuprous aluminum chloride (CuAlCl4) dissolved in an aromatic hydrocarbon solvent such as benzene or toluene. The aromatic-CuAlCl4 complex selectively targets carbon monoxide while being inert to other gases such as CO2, H2, N2, and CH4. These exhibit however some physical solubility in the aromatic solvent. H2O, H2S, SO2 and oxides of nitrogen are poisons to the complex [58]. Typical product recovery is 98%, with 99% purity [57]. Advantages of the process include: High purities and recoveries are achievable, no need for CO2, N2, H2, CH4 separation, and it can be applied to low CO concentration feeds. Drawbacks of the technology include [57,58]: extensive pretreatment is needed to remove H2O, H2S and other undesirable components, solvent degradation and precipitation of solids, and environmental issues related to disposal of spent solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lim et al. [60] compared the costs of the COSORB technology with an adsorption process for CO recovery from BOFG. The COSORB process was simulated in the Aspen Plus ® software for the production of a 10 000 Nm 3 /h stream with 99% CO purity from a 16 000 to 17 400 Nm 3 /h BOFG stream containing 60 -65% CO. 99% CO recovery was achieved. Absorption was simulated based on the experimental data presented by Hogendoorn et al. [61] by means of simple separators. CO product stream was compressed to 4.8 bars. Separation cost was estimated around 0.42 $/Nm 3 . A detailed list of cost parameters is available in English [60]. Absorbent price was estimated at 0.8 $/kg and absorbent losses were taken as 1.5%. Validation of these cost parameters for the COPURE technology should be performed before trying to determine separation costs for new units.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Physical absorption technologies
texts.text: 4.2. Physical absorption technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In physical absorption systems, gas uptake in the solvent is described by Henry ' s law, and there are no chemical reactions between the gas and the solvent. A two-step process like for chemical absorption is also used. Absorption is favored at high partial pressures of the targeted gas component in the gas phase and low temperatures. Desorption, and thus solvent regeneration, is favored at high temperatures and low pressures, so this step is carried out by heating or pressure reduction. Energy for this desorption stage is inferior compared to chemical absorption systems due to the absence of chemical bonding, and the operation under pressure reduction results in much lower energy consumption. Physical absorption systems are available for CO2 separation. No physical solvent systems are available for selective CO or H2 capture.

texts.text: 4.2.1. CO2 absorption technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Physical absorption can only be applied economically to gas streams with high CO2 partial pressures and it is often unadvised for CO2 capture from fl ue gas streams at ambient pressures [62]. It has been proposed for recovering CO2 from Integrated Gasi fi cation Combined Cycle (IGCC) systems where the CO2 would leave the gasi fi er at elevated pressures, 12 -20 bars partial pressure [63].

It has been proposed for recovering CO2 from Integrated Gasi fi cation Combined Cycle (IGCC) systems where the CO2 would leave the gasi fi er at elevated pressures, 12 -20 bars partial pressure [63].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the Iron and steel industry, only a couple of works have proposed a physical absorption for CO2 capture from BFG [64,65]. Both rely on gas compression to increase the partial pressure of CO2 necessary for the absorption stage. Gielen [64] proposed the treatment of the compressed BFG in a shift reactor to convert existing CO into additional CO2 and H2. CO2 capture using the Selexol process is then applied to this CO2-enriched high pressure stream to produce a stream ready for transportation and CO2 storage at 100 bars. The residual stream from the CO2 capture process, a pressurized N2-H2 mixture, is treated by membrane separation to recover an enriched H2 gas for power production, and a high pressure N2 residual gas for energy recovery in an expansion turbine. The process was proposed on a conceptual scale and process analysis and cost calculations were based on performance and cost factors from similar units in power plants. Total costs were estimated in the range of 23.1 -26 $/ton CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lampert et al. [65] studied in more detail a Selexol process for the CO2 capture from BFG, to enhance the lower heating value of the puri fi ed gas. In their process con fi guration, a CO2 stream is produced at 80 bars with 90% CO2 recovery. Depending of the absorption pressure, and thus compression energy requirements, power consumption of the process was estimated between 0.8 -1 GJ/ton CO2 for absorption pressures between 15 -40 bars. No economic analysis was presented.

texts.orig: 4.3. Adsorption technologies
texts.text: 4.3. Adsorption technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adsorption separation processes are based on the selective retention of a component from a gas mixture in the surface of a porous solid called the adsorbent. Depending on the nature of the gas-solid interactions, absorption is controlled by a chemical bonding (chemisorption) or by weaker physical forces (physisorption). As with absorption technology the process takes place in two steps, an adsorption step where the gas is put in contact with the solid under controlled temperature and/or pressure conditions, and a desorption step where the saturated adsorbent undergoes regeneration by liberating the separated gas. The regenerated adsorbent is ready for a new adsorption step, and the process takes place in a cyclic manner. Simultaneous operation of at least two adsorption beds, one operating under adsorption mode and one under regeneration, allows for a continuous separation process. Alternatively, the adsorbent can move between an adsorption section and a desorption section within the process, in a con fi guration known as moving-bed. In pressure swing adsorption (PSA), the adsorbent is

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3.1. CO2 and CO adsorption technologies
texts.text: 4.3.1. CO2 and CO adsorption technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chemical adsorbents for CO2 include amine-based adsorbents (amine grafted or impregnated solids), metal oxides, metal salts, double salts and hydrotalcites, while physical adsorbents include materials such as carbon-based materials, mesoporous silica, zeolites, zeolitic imidiazolate frameworks (ZIF ' s), metal-organic frameworks (MOF ' s), and blended adsorbents [67]. Both kinds have been studied vastly in the literature for CO2 capture applications [67 -69], however, to date there are no operating large-scale commercial CO2 capture processes based on adsorption technology [69]. CO selective adsorbents are much less abundant. Sodium-type mordenite as well as other zeolites, and active carbon supported copper are the most studied adsorbents [58]. Zeolite supported copper adsorbents have also been tested more recently [70].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The TGR-BF technology developed during the ULCOS program, relied on a VPSA process to separate a CO2 rich stream from the N2-lean BFG. The remaining CO-H2 rich mixture was recycled back to the BF. The process was assured by Air Liquide ' s VPSA technology. This technology was selected as it was judged to be the cheapest and simplest available solution at the time, allowing rapid technology evaluation [6]. 88% CO recovery was obtained and the recycled gas met the < 3% CO2 speci fi cation. The CO2 rich stream was to be further puri fi ed by a cryogenic process in order to meet purity speci fi cations for storage [71,72]. Costs of this PSA +cryogenics process were estimated at 27.5 -49.5 $/ton CO2 [72,73]. The energy consumption of the VPSA+compression and cryogenics process, producing a capture ready CO2 stream at a pressure of 110 bars, was evaluated at 1.05 GJ/ton CO2 vs 3.81 GJ/ton CO2 for an equivalent amine-based process [6]. Comparatively, a cost of 38 $/ton CO2 was estimated by Kim et al. [42] for CO2 recovery from a BFG with 30 -40% CO2 and (10 -20% N2), 90% CO2 recovery was achieved and cost includes CO2 product compression to 150 bars.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation of both CO2 and CO by adsorption was done in the fi rst commercial plant for CO recovery from BOFG developed by Kawasaki Steel Corporation and Osaka Oxygen Industries Ltd, Japan [74]. The objective was the production of a 98% CO rich stream from BOF gas (71% CO, 14% N2, 13% CO2 2% O2, H2O saturated) to a chemical plant for the production of trimellitic anhydride, a raw material in the production of high-grade plastics [74]. The process, called COPISA, consisted of a two-stage PSA process, fi rst a stage for CO2 removal by active carbon, and the second stage for CO recovery using sodium-type mordenite [74,75]. The fi rst PSA would remove practically all of the CO2 which was treated as a waste gas along with the N2-rich stream from the second PSA stage, charged with CO-N2 separation. CO recovery was around 45% for the production of a 98% purity product stream, for lower concentrations of 80 -85% CO, recovery of over 60% was possible [74]. No cost information was disclosed. Alternatively, a TSA process was considered by Ghanbari et al. [17], based on a patent by Rabo et al. [76]. Adsorbent used was a crystalline zeolitic molecular sieve having high SiO2/Al2O3 molar ratios and having monovalent copper cations as zeolite constituents. A 99% recovery was considered by the authors. No separation cost was detailed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mitsubishi Kakoki Kaishi also developed a PSA process for CO recovery from BOFG, in this case based on an active carbon supported copper adsorbent reported by Hirai et al. [77]. The adsorbent exhibited very high CO/CO2 selectivity and was not a ff ected by water vapor in the gas. It was however reversibly a ff ected by oxygen. A pilot unit was constructed and the process was tested for the separation of a producer gas with ∼ 37% CO. A 98% CO gas product with an 83% recovery was achieved. For a BOF gas with a higher CO concentration, > 60%, recovery was expected to increase to 95% for the same product purity, given that the residual gas concentration in the waste gas (regeneration gas) is independent on the partial pressure of the feed gas [78]. The separation cost for a unit producing 500 Nm 3 /h of CO was estimated at 0.83 $/Nm 3 CO [78]. A similar process was developed by Kobe steel, based on an activated alumina carrier using an impregnated carbon and copper compound as the active chemisorption species [79]. Pilot tests demonstrated the ability of the process to produce > 99% CO stream with 80 -90% recovery from BOFG with 68% CO content. Two commercial plants were constructed producing each 150 Nm 3 /h of CO product. A 2000 Nm 3 /h plant was designed based on positive results from the smaller plants.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Also in Japan, under the ongoing COURSE50 project, JFE steel is developing a PSA process for CO2 recovery from BFG, with an additional separation step for CO recovery. The selected adsorbent for CO2 was a zeolite, ZEOLUM F-9HA, which o ff ered the best working capacity (ratio of adsorbed gas to remaining gas after regeneration) and the best selectivity towards CO2 among several zeolites and activated carbon types tested [80]. CO2 recoveries just below 80% can be achieved for a 99% purity. Separation costs were in the order of 41 $/ton CO2. The cost target for the project was half of this value and cost reduction was achieved by shortening the cycle times, lowering adsorption pressure and considering the future use of BFG richer in CO2 (33 -35% vs 22% for lab tests). For these process conditions, CO2 recovery is around 80% for a 90% purity and a separation cost of ∼ 20 $/ton CO2. The process was demonstrated on a pilot plant (ASCOA-3) with a capacity of 3 tons CO2/ day. Estimated capital costs for a 1 × 10 6 tons CO2/year plant were around 72 M$ based on cost information presented by the authors [81]. The same ASCOA-3 pilot unit is considered for CO recovery from the BFG after the CO2 separation beds, however no information on the adsorbent used or process performance was detailed [82].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3.2. H2 adsorption technologies
texts.text: 4.3.2. H2 adsorption technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure swing adsorption is the leading technology for H2 recovery from COG [3,83,84]. Adsorbents used for this process include carbonaceous materials, alumina oxides and zeolites [3,85]. Since H2 is adsorbed much less strongly than the other species, a high H2 purity product, 99.99% and even 99.999% can be produced depending on feed H2 content and number of beds. Recoveries are in the order of 75% for four bed processes and up to 90% for modern multi-bed processes with higher capital costs [85,86]. Multiple beds are common in PSA processes for H2 recovery, each bed is made of di ff erent adsorbents layers with di ff erent a ffi nities to target speci fi c impurities in the feed gas such as water vapor, CO2, CH4 and CO [87]. PSA is preferred when concentrations of other components to be removed are more than a few percent [87] and gases containing less than 50% vol. H2 generally cannot be economically upgraded by PSA [85].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over 14 plants were constructed by Bergbau-Forschung Gmbh, producing up to 10,000 Nm 3 /h high purity hydrogen (99.999%) with recoveries up to 85%. Their PSA technology was based on the use of a wide-pore carbon molecular sieve as adsorbent [86]. UOP ' s Polybed TM PSA technology has also been applied to H2 production from COG. 19 plants have been constructed for this application with a capacity over 12,000 Nm 3 /h. Capacities over 250,000 Nm 3 /h have been installed for H2 production from other streams such as steam reforming gases [85]. No cost information of H2 recovery from COG is available in the open literature.

texts.orig: 4.4. Cryogenic technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of cryogenics for CO2 capture from fl ue gases is a relatively new idea [89] and it is generally not advised considering economic reasons for low CO2 streams such as in post-combustion fl ue gases but rather streams with higher CO2 concentrations such as those in oxy-fuel combustions scenarios [90]. It is often considered as a fi nal additional puri fi cation step while placed downstream of a complementary separation process responsible for CO2 enrichment as in the ULCOS VPSA+cryogenic process [71,72]. The main advantage of the cryogenic process is the absence of chemical systems and the operation at ambient pressure, besides the production of very pure liquid CO2 for transportation. Disadvantages are related to the costs of refrigeration as well as removal of water content in the feed to avoid plugging by ice or building of pressure loses [90].

texts.orig: H2 recovery from COG by cryogenic distillation is an alternative, and it o ff ers the advantage of high recoveries (90 -98%) with purities ranging 95 -99%. Disadvantages are related to high investments costs and variable product pressure [88]. As presented in the previous section, adsorption is the generally the preferred technology for H2 recovery from COG and most process streams.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Novel cryogenic processes are being developed for CO2 capture from postcombustion fl ue gases with interesting results [92 -94]. Song et al. [92] developed a process based on Stirling coolers and heat integration to reduce fi nal energy input. Process simulation of a fl ue gas at ambient temperature and 13% CO2 content, allowed 96% CO2 recovery and energy consumption was estimated at 1.5 GJ/ton CO2. Tuinier et al. [93] have proposed a new process based on dynamically operated packed beds. Required duty to recover over 99% CO2 from a fl ue gas containing 10% CO2 was estimated at 1.8 GJ/ton CO2 when cooling duty is provided by available lique fi ed natural gas. If the cooling must be supplied by refrigeration, more than 3.6 GJ/ton CO2 would be needed. The CCC TM process, developed by Sustainable Energy Solutions (SES), is based on the separation of CO2 as a solid after compression and cooling, and heat recovery from the cold products, including solid CO2, to cool the fl ue gas entering the process. Up to 99% CO2 recovery can be achieved with separation costs in the range of 14 -35 $/ton CO2 depending on plant integration [94]. These processes have not been yet evaluated for industrial emissions but if proved favorable in the future by commercial units, they might be interesting alternatives for CO2 recovery from steelmaking emissions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.5. Membrane permeation technologies
texts.text: 4.5. Membrane permeation technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas separation is based on the selective permeation of one component in a gas mixture across a membrane. The pressurized feed gas is put into contact with the surface of the membrane inside a membrane module conceived for the given application. Alternatively, vacuum can be applied on the other side of the membrane to create the driving force necessary for mass transport. Two outlet streams are recovered after treatment in a continuous process, a permeate stream, consisting of gas that has traversed the membrane and is thus enriched in the more permeable components, and a retentate stream, consisting in the residual gas that did not traverse the membrane and is hence enriched in the less permeable components. Membranes in the case of gas separation considered here consist mainly of dense layers with no porosity, unlike ultra fi ltration or nano fi ltration membranes, where separation capabilities are based on pore size. Although research is carried worldwide to develop novel membrane materials, only dense polymeric membranes are used commercially for gas separation at industrial scale. Transport in dense polymeric membranes is described by a solution-di ff usion mechanism. Generally speaking, the highest the solubility of a gas component in the polymer matrix, and the highest its di ff usion coe ffi cient, the more permeable it is.

Transport in dense polymeric membranes is described by a solution-di ff usion mechanism. Generally speaking, the highest the solubility of a gas component in the polymer matrix, and the highest its di ff usion coe ffi cient, the more permeable it is.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Advantages o ff ered by the membrane gas separation include: reduced environmental impact due to the absence of chemical systems; easy up and down scaling; ease of operation and control; no moving parts; low energy consumption since no additional energy is required besides the one required for gas fl ow; and low capital and operating costs. Common drawbacks are: need for multistage separations to achieve high purities and recoveries leading to higher capital and operating costs; interactions between some gas components under real fl ue gas conditions leading to lower performances than predicted by pure gas tests; and gas pretreatment can be heavy and thus expensive for some applications [95,96].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two kinds of membrane that could become interesting options in the near future for separation of gas components from steelmaking emissions are facilitated-transport membranes and metallic membranes. Facilitated transport membranes o ff er an active mass transport by means of a carrier species. This carrier present within the membrane transports the targeted permeating component across the membrane by forming complexes or other reversible species. This results in enhanced transport for the gas component reacting with the carrier. The carrier can be mobile, as for supported liquid membranes (SLM ' s) [91] or fi xed [97]. Drawbacks of this kind of membranes include the need for a humid feed since water is essential for the facilitated transport mechanism, membrane stability and carrier stability problems, and technical challenges for the development into industrial scale [98].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dense Palladium-based metallic membranes are being actively developed for production of high purity H2 [99,100], and commercial membrane units are available albeit only for production of H2 at small scale. One company o ff ers units treating up to 140 Nm 3 /h [101]. Advantages of metallic membranes are that they can operate at high temperatures (300 -600 °C) and o ff er the best H2 selectivities among membranes leading to purities over > 99.9999%. Drawbacks include an elevated cost and poisoning by H2S, HCl and CO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main industrial applications of polymer membranes are: production of N2 from air, removal of CO2, N2, H2O, H2S, and other hydrocarbons from natural gas, recovery of H2 from process streams, and vapor gas separations [96]. Membrane separation has been proposed by a few authors for separation of steelmaking emissions. These concern CO2 and H2 recovery applications as presented in the following sections. CO recovery applications are limited here due to the presence of N2, due to similar permeating characteristics as explained in the following chapter. SLM ' s are being developed for this application, however research is still at an early stage and it cannot be yet considered an alternative to traditional separation processes [91].

texts.text: 4.5.1. CO2 membrane separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lie et al. [97] evaluated at laboratory scale three types of membranes for CO2 recovery from BFG. Membranes evaluated included a semi-commercial adsorption selective carbon membrane, an in-house tailored carbon molecular sieving membrane, and a fi xed site carrier (FSC) membrane with amine groups in the polymer backbone of the membrane. The dense FSC membrane was found to o ff er the best permeance ( ∼ 18.5 GPU) and the best selectivities towards CO, N2 and H2 (>100) for pure gas tests. Experiments with real gas mixtures with a composition close to that of the average BFG, showed variable and mostly decreasing CO2 permeances (2 -21.9 GPU) and selectivities (<35). The authors claimed that the cause of this drop was later identi fi ed and solved.

Separation of both types of BFG required a second membrane stage when the low selectivity scenario was considered. The target purity of 90% CO2 was achieved in all cases with varying recoveries and estimated separation costs. CO2 recoveries and separation costs for the conventional BFG were 97% and 17.7 $/ton CO2 for pure gas selectivities, and 97% and 20.4 $/ton CO2 for low selectivities. No pilot testing was undertaken for this membrane for CO2 capture from steelmaking emissions. Recently, this membrane has been tested at pilot scale for CO2 capture from a coal fi red power plant [102] with positive results, and it is being tested on cement industry emissions [103].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Constant research e ff orts in the past decades have allowed the development of new polymeric membranes with better separation performances for CO2 especially from CO2-N2 mixtures [96,104]. Due to the important potential of CO2 emissions reduction by CO2 capture from fossil sources, membranes have been developed and tested in such scenarios. Rubbery polymers containing poly-ethylene oxide (PEO) have emerged as the most promising membrane materials [96]. Membrane Technology and Research (MTR) has developed a membrane, sold under the brand Polaris TM , with CO2 permeances ten times higher than conventional commercial CO2 membranes ( ∼ 1000 GPU) and a CO2/N2 selectivity among the highest reported for non-facilitated membranes [105]. This membrane has been tested for CO2 capture under real fl ue gas conditions at pilot scale with positive results, carbon capture rates better than 90% were achieved with coal-derived fl ue gas [106]. They have also been proposed for CO2 capture from syngas with low CO2 content ( ∼ 9% CO2) [107,108]. Another membrane, made from the commercial polymer PolyActive TM has been developed recently and tested for CO2 capture from gaseous hydrocarbon streams at pilot plant level with encouraging results [109].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, we presented the application of the Polaris TM membrane for CO2 recovery from BFG [110]. Separation cost for a two-stage membrane process was simulated based on pure-gas permeance data. Separation cost varied from 25.3 to 36.5 $/ton CO2 depending on CO2 recovery and residual N2 speci fi cations. Up to 99% CO2 recoveries could be achieved with an 81% CO2 purity, and up to 91% CO2 could be achieved for 90% CO2 recoveries. Around 4.5% of the CO2 product corresponded to water vapor. Mixed-gas permeance drops will increase estimated costs and laboratory tests are undergoing to measure these values. Conservative increases of the costs up to 40% would put this technology in the range of 35 -50 $/ton CO2.

texts.orig: 4.5.2. H2 membrane separation
texts.text: 4.5.2. H2 membrane separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: H2 recovery from process streams is a well-known membrane application, however there have been no reports on the application of membrane technology for H2 recovery from steelmaking emissions, namely COG, besides the work by Shen et al. [111]. In their work, a commercial PRISM membrane was tested for the production of a H2rich stream (> 90%) from a sample of real COG. The remaining CH4 rich stream was partially oxidized in a reactor for the production of syngas. The one stage membrane separation allowed the production of H2 with purities up to 95% for a 10 bars operation. CH4 rich stream can be produced with up to 70% CH4 under operation at lower pressures of 3 -5 bars. No economic analysis was presented.

Compared to PSA, membrane technology has the potential to o ff er lower maintenance costs (no switching valves) and better economics (lower price + faster deliveries, commissioning and start-up) [112,113].

texts.orig: 5. Outlook and future research perspectives
texts.text: 5. Outlook and future research perspectives

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Worldwide production of steel is currently dominated by ISM ' s which produce steel by the BF-BOF technology. Because of this coalbased primary steel production, the iron and steel sector occupies the fi rst place in direct CO2 emissions among industry. Multiple research programs are working to increase energy e ffi ciency and lower energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions trough di ff erent approaches: alternative reducing agents in the BF to lower CO2 emissions, capture and sequestration of CO2 from emissions, and innovative zerocarbon technologies to replace BF-BOF steel production.
texts.text: Worldwide production of steel is currently dominated by ISM ' s which produce steel by the BF-BOF technology. Because of this coalbased primary steel production, the iron and steel sector occupies the fi rst place in direct CO2 emissions among industry. Multiple research programs are working to increase energy e ffi ciency and lower energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions trough di ff erent approaches: alternative reducing agents in the BF to lower CO2 emissions, capture and sequestration of CO2 from emissions, and innovative zerocarbon technologies to replace BF-BOF steel production.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Commercial implementation of zero-carbon technologies is not expected before 2050, and thus carbon capture is necessary to meet CO2 reduction goals and mitigate the impact from steel production in the next decades. Carbon capture and storage (CCS) in the iron and steel industry has been evaluated by such research programs, such as ULCOS in Europe, which developed the TGR-BF technology, from which CO2 was captured by a VPSA process coupled with a cryogenic process from a gas fl ue gas richer in CO2. The technology was tested at pilot scale, however due to fi nancial, technological and legal risks no industrial demonstrator was constructed. In Japan, the COURSE50 project aims for both, a reduction of CO2 emissions in the blast furnace by the use of alternative reducing agents, and the capture of CO2 from these emissions by developing innovative low-cost chemical absorption and adsorption systems. texts.orig: Gas emissions from ISM ' s o ff er speci fi c opportunities when compared to fl ue gases from power generation due to the presence in varying compositions of CO and H2 along with the CO2 and N2 available in both cases. Main opportunities for recovery of these gases identi fi ed in this work are the following: recovery of CO2 from BFG, recovery of CO from BFG and/or BOFG, and recovery of H2 from COG. Absorption, adsorption and membrane technologies can all be of help for such application. Cryogenic separations seem to date more suited as an additional step following a previous treatment by one of the aforementioned processes if high purity from one gas component is needed and cost analysis support the additional cost. Table 5.1 presents a summary of the technologies presented in Section 4 as well as the key R&D areas identi fi ed in the following sections.

Main opportunities for recovery of these gases identi fi ed in this work are the following: recovery of CO2 from BFG, recovery of CO from BFG and/or BOFG, and recovery of H2 from COG. Absorption, adsorption and membrane technologies can all be of help for such application. Cryogenic separations seem to date more suited as an additional step following a previous treatment by one of the aforementioned processes if high purity from one gas component is needed and cost analysis support the additional cost. Table 5.1 presents a summary of the technologies presented in Section 4 as well as the key R&D areas identi fi ed in the following sections.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the most recent projects (2016 -2017) in the Iron & Steel Industry is the STEPWISE project. It aims to demonstrate at pilot scale the Sorption-Enhanced Water-Gas Shift (SEWGS) technology for CO2 capture from BFG [116]. The overall process consists of two units. First, a water gas shift of the compressed BFG is performed on a catalytic converter to provide a bulk transformation of the available CO into CO2 and H2. Second, the resulting CO2-H2 rich gases are sent to a SEWGS reactor column where both further conversion of the remaining CO and separation of the available CO2 and H2 are performed. This reactive separation is based on a hydrotalcite-based sorbent which is water gas shift active thus allowing the CO transformation to reach equilibrium while adsorbing CO2 and other acid gas components such as H2S. The SEWGS technology allows the production of a hot H2-rich product stream as CO2 is being adsorbed, and a CO2-rich product stream when the sorbent is depressurized and fl ushed with superheated steam. The process operates in a cyclic manner similarly to conventional PSA processes. If the SEWGS process is proved economical, it would o ff er a very interesting alternative to conventional converter-separator processes [116].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.1. Recovery of CO2 from BFG
texts.text: 5.1. Recovery of CO2 from BFG

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If very high purity CO2 (>99% CO2) is to be produced from BFG, chemical absorption is typically the fi rst alternative to be considered. Newly developed chemical absorbents with lower regeneration energies and temperatures have reduced the energy penalty associated with conventional amine processes. They have also enabled the use of low temperature waste heat for the desorption step which takes advantage of the availability of such resource in most ISM ' s [117]. Available absorbents systems include demixing solvents [56], blended synthetic amines [52], and aqueous ammonia [46]. Research e ff orts from a material engineering point of view are then to be focused on the improvement of the newly developed absorbents or on the identi fi cation of new absorbent systems. In both cases key issues are: lower regeneration energy penalties and/or lower regeneration temperatures, higher CO2 capture capacities, as well as improved physical-chemical characteristics a ff ecting handling, environmental and safety considerations. From a process point of view, demonstrator scale units are necessary to obtain process performance data to validate large scale application of the technologies and to o ff er a more accurate cost evaluation in such conditions.

In both cases key issues are: lower regeneration energy penalties and/or lower regeneration temperatures, higher CO2 capture capacities, as well as improved physical-chemical characteristics a ff ecting handling, environmental and safety considerations. From a process point of view, demonstrator scale units are necessary to obtain process performance data to validate large scale application of the technologies and to o ff er a more accurate cost evaluation in such conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adsorption systems have been reported to be able to provide high purity CO2 although with lower recoveries than for absorption systems, 60 -80% compared to > 90% for absorption [82]. Separation costs could be lower than for some absorption systems as presented in Section 4.2.1 [80]. Research e ff orts for development of this technology should address the following issues: Improvement of CO2 sorption capacity of adsorbents while retaining selectivity, process cycle optimization, and construction and operation of demonstrator units for potential adsorbent systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If very high CO2 purities are not necessary, membrane separation could o ff er more competitive costs than both absorption and adsorption technologies. Reduction of purity speci fi cations allows for increases in CO2 recoveries resulting in reduction of separation costs per ton of CO2 [110]. This fl exibility of CO2 speci fi cations is not possible for chemical absorption technologies. CO2 recoveries at low purity seem to be higher for membrane separation than for adsorption. Increases in recovery by adsorption can be achieved by increasing cycle time but only to a certain point, and cost rises at higher cycle times [82]. Membrane separation on the other hand can deliver higher purity CO2 at higher recoveries by multi-stage processes, however at increasing cost. Both technologies should be considered for the recovery of CO2 when a very high purity (> 99% CO2) is not the main concern.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.2. Recovery of CO from BFG and/or BOFG
texts.text: 5.2. Recovery of CO from BFG and/or BOFG

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recovery of CO from BFG or BOFG would rely at present on two technologies: chemical absorption by the commercial COPURE SM process, and adsorption separation by a PSA or TSA process. Chemical absorption allows the production of high purity CO ( ≥ 99%) at high recoveries ( ≥ 98%) [57]. Adsorption processes have reported similar purity but with lower recoveries depending on the adsorbent and process conditions [74,78,79,118]. Few recent cost information is available for these technologies, particularly for the absorption processes. Ghanbari et al. [17] chose a TSA process over the COPURE TM process in their cost optimized process fl owsheet. PSA processes have been more studied and the ability to regenerate the adsorbent by pressure reduction than by high temperature heating (300 °C) is an operational advantage that should also lead to lower process costs. Furthermore, PSA operation has been the approach taken by ongoing research e ff orts in the COURSE50 project [82] and some companies have developed this process at commercial level [118,119].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The COPURE SM process is the result of innovation applied to previous COSORB ® processes and further improvement of the technology is not likely to produce signi fi cant changes in process performance and cost. Development of new absorbent systems would allow to tackle the main drawbacks of the technology, related to solvent degradation by H2O and other components, solvent regeneration energy, and environmental concerns arising from the chemistry of the solvent system [120]. Chlorocuprate(I)-Based Ionic Liquids have been proposed recently as a promising solvent system for carbon monoxide recovery from waste gas streams of industrial processes [120]. Research on adsorption processes is directed to develop new adsorbent systems [121] or to process optimization of existing adsorbents [70,118].

texts.orig: 5.3. Recovery of H2 from COG
texts.text: 5.3. Recovery of H2 from COG

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PSA is the established commercial technology for H2 recovery from COG and allows for the production of very pure product streams (>99.9%) with up to 90% recoveries [85,86]. Research in this fi eld should be pointed towards process integration and optimization within gas valorization designs to improve the pro fi tability of H2 recovery from this source.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Concerning membrane separation, high H2 permeability membranes that could fi nd application on this separation are commercially available. Shen et al. [111] have presented positive results using a one stage separation with a PRISM ® membrane, purities up to 95% H2 were achieved but with lower recoveries than for a PSA process. Higher purities and recoveries can be achieved with multistage processes. Coste ff ectiveness of such an alternative and benchmarking with an alternative PSA process must be performed considering purity and recovery constraints of the recovered H2. - Very selective separation allows high purity (>99%) product streams even at high recoveries (>99%) - Vast operational experience for CO 2 recovery from process streams with conventional solvents - Recovery of free waste heat by energy integration to produce lower separation costs - Active research fi eld for this and other big volume emission sources (cement, power plants)

- High energy consumption for conventional solvent systems - Environmental impact of solvents and process safety issues related to chemical nature of solvents - Large process footprint for treatment of large emissions for solvent with low CO 2 loading

- Application of traditional amine solvents has been evaluated as too expensive with costs > 60 € /ton CO 2 [42,56] - New proprietary solvents have been developed and have been tested from laboratory to small pilot scale with positive results [53] - Demonstrator plants planned for some new solvents < 2020 [22,53]

- Development of new chemical solvent systems with improved characteristics: higher CO 2 loading capacity, lower or inexistent corrosion and degradation rates - Decrease in solvent regeneration energy for new systems below the 4 GJ/ton CO 2 MEA benchmark. A value of ∼ 2 GJ/ton CO 2 has been proposed, and some solvent systems have achieved this value [53] - Decrease in regeneration temperature below the 120 °C MEA benchmark.
